text:
Hunter × Hunter: Nen × Impact is a 2.5D fighting game developed by Eighting and published in Japan by Bushiroad Games and internationally by Arc System Works. It was released on July 17 th , 2025, for the PlayStation 5, Nintendo Switch, and Microsoft Windows (via Steam).

Overview

Hunter × Hunter: Nen × Impact is a video game adaptation of Yoshihiro Togashi’s Hunter × Hunter franchise. Designed as both a competitive fighting experience and a story-driven adventure, the game allows players to explore the mechanics of Nen through fast-paced battles and narrative-driven missions. Featuring a roster of iconic characters, multiple gameplay modes, and an emphasis on faithful representation of Nen abilities, Nen Impact blends strategic combat with the series’ unique power system, offering both fans and newcomers a chance to experience the world of Hunters in an interactive form.

Game Content

Hunter × Hunter: Nen Impact offers both single-player and multiplayer experiences, combining story-driven content, competitive battles, and training features. Players can progress through endurance-based challenges, boss encounters, and performance trials, or test their speed and combo execution in specialized missions. Multiplayer options include both online matchmaking and local battles with customizable settings, while practice tools let players refine skills in controlled environments. Additional features allow rewatching past matches and collecting unlockable items, rounding out the game’s mix of progression, competition, and replayability.

Game Modes

Online Mode: Play against others worldwide through an internet connection. Ranked matches allow searching for opponents with a specific set of conditions. Players can directly challenge friends or chosen rivals for more personal matchups. This mode blends competitive progression with flexible play options.
Free Battle: Play against others locally or against the CPU. Battle is customizable.
Training Mode: Practice and hone their skills on any playable character.
Single Mode: A collection of difficulty-varying sub-modes, including:
Arcade: A gauntlet of eight battles where the final fight is against Meruem, who has significantly boosted health. Difficulty can be set to Easy, Normal, or Hard.
Heavens Arena: A 31-stage endurance climb where health doesn't reset between battles. Players earn Fight Money based on performance, which can be spent to restore health. With no checkpoints, survival and resource management are key.
Battle Olympia: An advanced mode unlocked by clearing Heavens Arena. It features eight matches against very strong opponents. Winning grants exclusive rewards.
Time Attack: A speed-based challenge where players must defeat five stages as quickly as possible, encouraging fast and efficient play.
Combo Trial: A mission-style challenge where players must execute specific combos on a non-fighting Leorio dummy to complete objectives and test technical skill.
Replay: Rewatch matches.
Collection: View collected items.
Story Mode: Iconic scenes from the 2011 anime adaptation return as stages, combining cutscenes with battles against CPU opponents. Clearing stages or completing missions rewards the player, while certain stages also act as tutorials to teach core gameplay mechanics. Mission completion often have time limits and often require the execution of certain moves.

Gameplay

In the game, players use a set of light, medium, and heavy attacks, along with directional command moves and special abilities that vary by character. A universal action button handles charged stances, unique attacks, and counter options. There are also special move buttons for character-specific abilities and super moves that consume a resource gauge. Assist buttons call in teammates for temporary support attacks, and a combo/auto-attack button allows automated attack sequences. Directional inputs modify attacks, and most moves can chain, launch, or cancel into others to create extended combos. The layout adapts to each platform while maintaining consistency in these functions.

Movement: A set of basic actions the character can perform. Dashing can be done either by tapping a direction twice or by tapping multiple normal attack buttons at once. Ground dashes can also be cancelled into an attack or a crouch.
Normals: A set of basic attack actions that is further split into three categories: Light, Medium, and Heavy. Crouching Heavy Attacks often sweep the victim off their feet when they connect for most characters, and most jumping Heavy Attacks also may inflict a universal plummet.
Normals usually are easily cancellable (on hit or on block) not only into each other via a magic series/chain combo routing with lighter-ones progressing into stronger-ones, but also into higher-tiered actions such as Specials, Nenpact Attacks, Arts, and Aura Arts. As a rule of thumb between normal attacks:
Light normals are all cancel-able into either medium or heavy normals.
Medium normals are only cancel-able into heavy normals.
However, there also exists a move-chain-limit that prevents any further normal attacks from being input within the chain; most characters can only chain 6 total different normals within the sequence of this limit. Pushback that causes the victim to be pushed out of range is also to be considered since it can cause incoming chained normals or moves in general to whiff.
Specials: Also called Forte Techniques (得意技, Tokui Waza) or "Unique Attacks/Command Normals/Lever-Input Arts". Characters also have a set of additional normal moves that are unique to each character. Details about a character's Specials can be found in the Action List when the game is paused. Generally, Normals can cancel into Specials, and Specials can cancel into Arts, but exceptions exist.
For the most part when it comes to chaining, Forte Techniques/Specials can be chained into freely, but only within the button ranges of the Forte Technique/Special in question:
Medium-buttoned Specials can only be chained-into off of any light or normal attacks.
Heavy-buttoned Specials on the other hand, can be chained into off of any range of normals.
However, most Forte Techniques/Specials CANNOT bypass the aforementioned move-chain-limit.
Nenpact Attacks: A set of powerful attacks that utilize Nen. They are usually executed as a follow-up attack to a Nen Stance, except jumping Nenpact Attacks. Grounded standing Nenpact Attacks tend to knock away their victims or stagger them, while crouching Nenpact Attacks are universal launcher attacks (that are all cancel-able into a jump). Jumping/Midair Nenpact Attacks vary, being either diving attacks or plummet finishers to basic air combo chains. Grounded Nenpact Attacks can also bypass the Nen Stance input by simply being chain-cancelled into by any moves (mainly Normals) that are cancel-able into Nenpact Attacks in general.
When it comes to chaining and reaching aforementioned move-chain-limit of a sequence, Nenpact Attacks can bypass this restriction entirely.
Arts: The game's take on the recurring Special Moves/Death Blows (必殺技, Hissatsu Waza; lit. "Sure-Killing Arts") mechanic in many fighting games. A set of powerful moves that rely on a character's unique characteristics. Controls vary between characters. Details about of a character's Arts can be found in the Action List when the game is paused.
Arts for the most part, can also be chain-cancelled into off any normal, Forte Technique or Nenpact Attack, even if the result may not necessarily combo.
Aura Arts: A set of super-powerful special moves that consume a varying amount of the "Aura Gauge", being the game's take on the traditional super move/attack. Upon landing, a substantial amount of damage will be dealt to the opponent. All characters have a Level 1 and 3 Aura Art, and some have a Level 2. The level of the Aura Art reflects the amount of aura (bars on the Aura Gauge) consumed.
Just like standard Arts, they too can be normally cancelled into off of any normals, Forte Techniques or Nenpact Attacks; however, certain standard Arts can also be chain-cancelled into Aura Arts likewise.
Guard: A set of defensive actions that catches and reduces the amount of damage dealt by the opponent. Normal attacks can be completely blocked, though blocking Arts and Aura Arts that inflict damage will instead inflict "chip damage" upon the defender that is reduced from their usual amount landed on hit. While not indicated, guarding also has limited durability to the point where if a defender blocks too many attacks, they will end up guard-crushed. Some attacks however are also outright unblockable.
Nen Stance: An omnidirectional defensive stance that uses Aura as fuel, creating extra pushback upon the attacker and also negating incoming chip damage from Arts and Aura Arts (as well as preventing a guard crush). To activate, hold "N" or one of its equivalents (X in PS5, A in Xbox, B in Nintendo Switch). Can only trigger it in a standing state (crouching Nen Stance is not possible), and double-tapping N triggers a Nenpact Attack. However, since inputting N when chaining it from a cancel-able attack also causes an Nenpact Attack to come out, Nen Stances can only be used when not attacking or when not in midair. Unblockable attacks are still unblockable even with a Nen Stance.
Piercing Attack: An attack that can be used to either bypass an opponent's defenses or counter the opponent's usage of a Piercing Attack, acting as a traditional universal throw attack. The user throws a guard-crushing body blow followed straight into an attack. It can be activated by dashing forward or by pressing ← /→ along with H or one of its equivalents (◯ in PS5, B in Xbox, A in Nintendo Switch) when right next to the opponent, and can also activate in midair (only against an airborne opponent at roughly the same height). A number of characters primarily use either their Heavy or Nenpact Attack animations for their Piercing Attacks, with roughly the same varied properties as the original attack animation used.
Assist Attack: It is an attack executed by a reserve character. To activate using the first reserve character, press A1 or one of its equivalents (R1 for PS5, RB for Xbox, R for Nintendo Switch); to activate using the second reserve character, press A2 or one of its equivalents (L1 for PS5, LB for Xbox, L for Nintendo Switch). The attack has a cooldown.
Switch: Switches the current character with one from the reserve. Press the button to trigger the Assist Attack for the desired reserve and press the same button as the assisting character is attacking to switch with them.
Switch Arts: A set of actions that switches the current character with one of the reserves instantly while launching an Aura Arts followup attack. Upon usage, one Aura Gauge is consumed, and a cooldown is initiated. To activate, Combo-press N (X in PS5, A in Xbox, B in Nintendo Switch) with A1 (R1 for PS5, RB for Xbox, R for Nintendo Switch) to switch with the second character, or N with A2 (L1 for PS5, LB for Xbox, L for Nintendo Switch) for the third character. Switch Arts cannot be triggered on any connected Level 3 Aura Arts.
Reversal Switch: An action to switch instantly with a reserve character in an emergency. Upon usage, one Aura Gauge is consumed. To activate, Combo-press → with A1 (R1 for PS5, RB for Xbox, R for Nintendo Switch) to switch with the second character. Alternatively, to switch with the third character, use A2 (L1 for PS5, LB for Xbox, L for Nintendo Switch) instead of A1.
Ejection Attack: An attack that forces the opponent to switch with one of their reserves by literally booting them off the screen. It can only be used during a non-Level 3 Aura Art and consumes one extra Aura Gauge level. To activate, Combo-press ←/→ with A1 (R1 for PS5, RB for Xbox, R for Nintendo Switch) to switch with the second character. Alternatively, to switch with the third character, use A2 (L1 for PS5, LB for Xbox, L for Nintendo Switch) instead of A1.
Rush: The primary autocombo mechanic of the game, where holding the Rush button down (L2 in PS5, LT in Xbox, ZL in Nintendo Switch) and pressing any normal attack button continuously forms an automatic combo for the user that even ends in an Aura Arts cancel. However, it can be toggled on or off during setup by unassigning it.
Quick Gear: A technique that utilizes the Quick Gear Gauge, which, when activated, will slowly drain it in return for a strengthened attack power, speed, and a perfect guard until it fully drains or the opponent successfully lands an attack. It is activated by pressing N (X on PS5, A on Xbox, B on Nintendo Switch) and L (◻ on PS5, X on Xbox, Y on Nintendo Switch). Can be utilized as a safe "anywhere" cancel to return to neutral for safely cancelling sluggish attacks or to create unique combo extensions.
Over Gear: A powerful technique that achieves the same beneficial effects as Quick Gear with some caveats. It increases the boost based on the level of the Quick Gear Gauge, and its effect stacks up with Quick Gear. It also knocks the opponent back upon activation. However, it can only be used once per battle, and it requires a Gear level above one. To activate it, press N (X in PS5, A in Xbox, B in Nintendo Switch) along with H (◯ in PS5, B in Xbox, A in Nintendo Switch). Like Quick Gear, Over Gear also can be utilized for similar cancel mechanics.

Trivia

Nen Impact shares a normal chain-combo/magic series style as both Tatsunoko vs. Capcom and Marvel vs. Capcom 3, two other tag-fighting games earlier also developed by 8ing.
Like the two other games (and replicated by the Bleach DS fighting games developed by Treasure), normals with the usual magic series-routing of lightest-to-strongest are limited by a zig-zag of being unable to go from a same-strength-crouching normal to a same-strength-standing normal. As a result, crouching normals can only chain into stronger-tiered normals right after. The full possible chain in theory is standing L>crouching L>standing M>crouching M>standing H>crouching H, provided each move connects properly on hit and/or block.
However, like Marvel vs. Capcom 3, there exists a "move-chain-limit" where certain characters cannot chain more than a certain amount of normals or Forte Techniques in their magic series/chain combo before their inputs cease to register briefly. But also like MvC3, Nenpact Attacks function akin to how the S button works in that they are universal attacks that can bypass the said limit.
The three games also share many other assets, including the ability to plink-dash/wavedash (carried over by the Marvel vs. Capcom games in general), by continuously crouch-cancelling grounded dashes (input by the macro command of multiple normal attack buttons), and maintaining a steady rhythm to not accidentally attack mid-way.
Ejection Attacks are essentially Nen Impact 's take on the Superhero vs. Capcom series' Snapback mechanic (physically booting-away the current on-point victim to force a switch-in to one of their partners), while Switch Arts are a take on the Delayed Hyper Combo mechanic (switching into a partner's super attack during the current on-point user's own).
Meruem and Biscuit in her True Form are the only two characters to have the ability to "reverse chain" their normal attacks from stronger-to-weaker strength, but with some limitations. Biscuit's True Form specifically lowers her possible move chain limit to make her chain combos shorter as a trade-off.
To account for the small body sizes of the main cast, most of Gon's, Killua's, and Kurapika's normals inch them forward to a notable degree, unlike more stationary normal attacks across most fighting game casts in 2D fighters.
Due to Kurapika's jumping Heavy Attack being a dive alongside his jumping Nenpact Attack involving his chain, he uniquely uses his jumping Medium Attack for his midair Piercing Attack animation.
Due to the unavailability of their 2011 anime adaptation voice actors, Leorio (whose voice actor Keiji Fujiwara has since passed away) and Neferpitou (whose voice actor Ayumi Fujimura has since retired) use archived voice recordings.
This is not the first time the SuperSweep Company has composed the soundtrack to a fighting game developed by 8ing based on an IP from Shōnen Jump magazine; the other said IP happens to be the Naruto series via the Gekitō Ninja Taisen/Clash of Ninja series.
